records.capability_statement: AOF can now make release value comprehension itself a governed runtime outcome instead of relying on after-the-fact AI summaries.
records.before_state: Users had to infer value from records, audits, schemas, gates, projections, and long release histories.
records.after_state: Users can read capability-first release value evidence, a before/after comparison, a five-minute demo, and Mission Control understanding projection.
records.scenario: A user asks what changed from v9.0 to v9.1 and can answer from the capability matrix before reading implementation details.
records.five_minute_demo: Open docs/v9.1-product-value-comprehension.md, read the capability matrix, then run product-value-evidence-audit to verify the value evidence exists.

records.capability_statement: Operators can inspect whether provider production execution is merely a candidate, blocked, or missing required pre-production boundaries before any real provider operation is considered.
records.before_state: AOF had approval, reproduction, rollback, outcome, learning, operator acceptance, and value evidence, but no single boundary packet explained whether production execution was eligible.
records.after_state: AOF has a provider production boundary candidate packet and audit that bundles upstream evidence and blocks production execution authorization in v9.2.
records.scenario: Before attempting a real GitHub/Jira/Slack/OpenAI provider write, an operator runs provider-production-boundary-audit and sees credential, budget, revocation, rollback, monitoring, incident, human go/no-go, and value-comprehension boundaries.
records.five_minute_demo: Open docs/v9.2-release-definition.md, then run provider-production-boundary-audit and release-state-audit; confirm production_execution_authorized_count is 0.

records.capability_statement: Users can judge an AOF release by capability first instead of inferring value from records, audits, schemas, dashboards, or tags.
records.before_state: AOF could require product value evidence, but release value was still not formalized as a versioned product capability model.
records.after_state: AOF has a product capability register, capability matrix, capability delta, and release audit that requires release notes to start from user capability.
records.scenario: A user opens v9.3 and wants to know in 30 seconds what changed from v9.2.
records.five_minute_demo: Open docs/aof-capability-matrix.md, read the v9.3 row, then run capability-first-release-audit.

records.capability_statement: AOF can present a provider-backed action as one controlled execution candidate with go/no-go status and a production-not-authorized boundary.
records.before_state: Provider readiness evidence existed across many artifacts and had to be mentally assembled.
records.after_state: Operator can inspect one controlled execution candidate and audit result before go/no-go.
records.scenario: AOF is considering a bounded GitHub issue creation provider-backed action.
records.five_minute_demo: Run provider-controlled-execution-candidate-audit and inspect PCEC-TASK-114-V94 to see upstream refs, stop conditions, and production authorization=false.

records.capability_statement: Operators can inspect provider incident recovery before advancing a provider-backed action.
records.before_state: AOF could show a controlled provider execution candidate, but failure handling was mostly boundary text.
records.after_state: AOF records detection, containment, rollback decision, recovery action, operator notification, learning update, and resume/stop governance as auditable recovery evidence.
records.scenario: A GitHub issue creation candidate receives an unexpected provider response; AOF stops calls, preserves evidence, checks rollback readiness, notifies the operator, and defers resume until governance review.
records.five_minute_demo: Run provider-incident-recovery-audit and release-state-audit from a clean checkout.

checks.name: PVE-TASK-111-V91 not-proven boundary
